evidence: The highest scores reflect 'erroneous' as fundamentally tied to identifying incorrect information (epistemological.identification 10), operating at the data/information layer (ontological.informational 10), and embodying the negative counterpart to correctness (symmetry.destructive 10). Lower scores highlight its contingent nature (lawfulness.contingent 3), potential to propagate errors (interconnection.chained 2), and ethical/functional consequences (value_generation 5 each).

Fallacious strongly resonates with semantic meaning (VAL.incorrect), structural patterns in reasoning, and ethical implications of being 'wrong.' It operates at individual/community/society scales, influences over time (establishment/continuation), and connects through catalytic/chained cause-effect relationships. Its essence touches core value judgments (ethical) and informational layers.

False strongly resonates with semantic meaning (ontological.semantic), ethical implications (value_generation.ethical), and destructive impact (symmetry.destructive). It operates at societal scale (scale.society) and involves understanding incorrectness (epistemological.understanding). Lower scores reflect its indirect connections to structural patterns (resonance.structural) and contingent contexts (lawfulness.contingent).

Falseness strongly resonates with semantic layers (ontological.semantic) and ethical value (value_generation.ethical), as it directly concerns meaning and moral judgment. It also aligns with destructive symmetry (symmetry.destructive) as the inverse of correctness, and influences through catalytic/chained interconnections (e.g., propagating errors). Essential resonance (resonance.essential) reflects its core role in defining truth's opposite.

Falsity strongly resonates with identification (labeling incorrectness), semantic (value of truth), essential (core of truth/falsehood), destructive (opposite of correct), comprehension (understanding errors), and ethical (right/wrong judgments). Lower scores reflect its role in information, individual/community scales, and contingent lawfulness.

The word 'ill-formed' strongly resonates with structural and essential aspects of incorrectness (e.g., flawed structures, core deviations from correctness). It also connects to destructive symmetry (as a pathology of correctness) and ethical value (moral wrongness). Scores are low in areas requiring physicality, universality, or positive creation, aligning with its contrast to VAL.correct.

The highest scores reflect 'improper's core semantic resonance (10 in ontological.semantic and resonance.essential), its epistemological identification (7) and understanding (5), and its ethical implications (9). Scale.individual (3) acknowledges personal wrongdoing. Symmetry.destructive (7) captures its opposition to 'correct'.

The highest scores reflect 'inconstant' as a semantic issue (ontological.semantic) tied to meaning/value, its destructive opposition to 'correct' (symmetry.destructive), and its unpredictable nature (lawfulness.emergent). Moderate scores align with understanding errors (experience.comprehension), moral implications (value_generation.ethical), and its influence over time (temporal.influence).

Incorrect strongly resonates with epistemological identification (5) and understanding (4) as it involves labeling and grasping factual errors. Ontological informational (8) and semantic (5) reflect its core tie to factual truth. Symmetry destructive (5) captures its negative impact, while resonance essential (5) ties to truth's core. Scale scores reflect its applicability from individual to societal levels.

Incorrectness strongly resonates with factual inaccuracies (ontological.informational), individual mistakes (scale.individual), and destructive consequences (symmetry.destructive). It weakly connects to identification of errors (epistemological.identification) and cause-effect chains (interconnection.chained). Ethical implications are rare unless contextually tied to wrongdoing.

Mendacious strongly resonates with ontological.informational (10) as it directly pertains to factual inaccuracies. It aligns with symmetry.destructive (7) due to its inherently negative, untruthful nature. Value.ethical (8) reflects its moral dimension of wrongness. Lower scores reflect its localized impact (scale.individual/community/society) and core resonance with truth's absence (resonance.essential).

Misguided strongly resonates with semantic layers (ontological.semantic) and ethical value (value_generation.ethical), as it implies a wrongness tied to meaning and morality. It also connects to structural patterns (resonance.structural) and destructive dynamics (symmetry.destructive), reflecting errors rooted in flawed reasoning that persist over time (temporal.influence, temporal.continuation).

Mistaken strongly resonates with scale.individual (5) as it often describes personal errors. It aligns with ontological.informational (5) and ontological.semantic (5) due to factual inaccuracies and value judgments. interconnection.chained (3) reflects propagation through cause-effect sequences. Lower scores reflect limited connections to broader scales or existential aspects.

The highest scores reflect 'specious' as a deceptive incorrectness (symmetry.destructive 8), rooted in meaning/value (ontological.semantic 10), with ethical implications (value.ethical 9). It resonates structurally with truth's essence (resonance.essential 5) and can catalyze errors (interconnection.catalytic 2).

Spurious strongly resonates with semantic value judgments (ontological.semantic) and epistemological identification of error. It operates across individual/community/society scales and catalyzes influence through misinformation. Its destructive symmetry and ethical implications align with VAL.incorrect's opposition to correctness.

The word 'true' in the context of incorrectness strongly resonates with semantic layers (meaning/value being wrong), destructive symmetry (as a pathology), and ethical implications. It also connects to contingent conditions (dependence on context), discovery of error, and comprehension of its consequences.

The term 'trumped-up' strongly resonates with ontological.semantic (core meaning of incorrectness), symmetry.destructive (pathological falsehood), and value_generation.ethical (moral wrongness). It also connects to interconnection.chained (propagating falsehoods) and lawfulness.contingent (context-dependent wrongness).

Ungrammatical strongly resonates with ontological layers (informational, structural, semantic) as it violates grammatical rules. It touches the essential core of correctness (resonance.essential) and reflects destructive symmetry (pathology). It operates at an individual scale and relies on identification/experience of errors.

The word 'untrue' strongly resonates with semantic slots tied to its core meaning of factual inaccuracy. High scores reflect its connection to ethical judgment (value.ethical), opposition to correctness (symmetry.destructive), and its essence as a negation of truth (resonance.essential). Moderate scores align with individual/societal scale impacts and epistemological processes of identification and understanding. Most slots score 0 as they lack direct relevance to the atom's definition.

The word 'wrong' strongly resonates with semantic meaning (core to VAL's incorrectness) and ethical value (moral dimension of 'right/wrong'). It also connects to destructive symmetry (as the inverse of correctness) and essential resonance (touching the essence of validity). Scores for identification (naming incorrectness) and comprehension (understanding errors) reflect its epistemological role.

Wrongness strongly resonates with semantic (core value layer), ethical (right/wrong dimension), and identification (naming errors). It operates at individual/community scales and informational layers (data errors). Weak connections exist to temporal influence (consequences over time), epistemological perception (noticing errors), and symmetry.destructive (as a pathology).
